---
title: "Borrowed Plumes: A Holy Constitution and Its Unholy Judges"
subtitle: "A Proposal for Democratising Constitutional Review"
author:
- name: "Dianyi Yang" 
  orcid: "0009-0004-4652-3429"
date: '2026-07-14'
# categories:
#   - Reproducibility
#   - R
#   - Quarto
# image: featured.jpg
google-scholar: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

A recent Financial Times article reports that Justice Amy Coney Barrett once compared serving on the US Supreme Court to “being in an arranged marriage with no option of divorce”. The comparison now appears unusually apt. During the Court’s latest term, its members interrupted one another, exchanged personal criticisms, and accused colleagues of advancing arguments that were “baseless and insulting” [@lee2026]. The ideological polarisation that has long characterised American electoral and mass politics has now become increasingly visible within the country’s highest court.

What makes the judicial branch distinctive is not that judges are somehow less political than other public officials. It is that constitutional politics surrounds them with a sacred veil. Their decisions are represented not as exercises of public power, but as declarations of what *“the law”* or *“the Constitution”* has already commanded. Political choices disappear behind legal language. A conclusion reached by five judges against four is transformed into the meaning of the Constitution, as though the document itself had spoken.

> The Constitution may be treated as holy. Its interpretation need not be—and the people who interpret it certainly are not.

The justices’ increasingly public disagreements offer an opportunity to look behind this veil of judicial professionalism. Courts should be understood not as secular temples whose occupants merely transmit constitutional truth, but as fallible institutions exercising enormous public power. Like other governing institutions, they are susceptible to ideological conflict, strategic behaviour, personal bias and error.

Legal expertise remains indispensable. But expertise does not make judges infallible, nor does it convert every contested judicial choice into constitutional necessity.

This is why I propose a reform of constitutional review.

## Example: Roe, Dobbs, and the politics of constitutional interpretation

Perhaps no case better illustrates the limits of judicial supremacy than *Roe v. Wade* (1973) and its reversal in *Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization* (2022).

The Constitution did not change between 1973 and 2022.

The text of the Fourteenth Amendment remained exactly the same. The relevant constitutional provisions were identical. Yet the constitutional meaning attributed to those provisions shifted dramatically. In Roe, the Supreme Court held that the Due Process Clause protected a constitutional right to abortion as part of a broader right to privacy. Nearly half a century later, Dobbs rejected that conclusion and held that the Constitution conferred no such right, returning the issue to the political process [@roe1973; @dobbs2022].

If constitutional meaning can reverse so completely while the constitutional text remains unchanged, it becomes difficult to maintain that the Court is merely discovering what the Constitution has always meant. Constitutional interpretation is not simply an exercise in textual retrieval. It is an exercise in constitutional judgment.

Defenders of judicial review often present *Dobbs* as evidence that the Court had become politicised. But *Roe* offers the clearer example of politics presented as constitutional interpretation.

The Constitution contains no express right to privacy, still less an express right to abortion. *Roe* nevertheless derived a constitutional right to abortion from the liberty protected by the Due Process Clause and then constructed a detailed trimester framework governing when states could regulate it. That reasoning is difficult to describe as the interpretation of a rule already contained in the constitutional text. The distance between the text and the result is simply too great.

The structure of the reasoning is instead *ex post*. The desired conclusion—that women should possess a legally protected right to abortion—comes first; the search for a constitutional provision capable of supporting that conclusion comes afterwards. The privacy doctrine supplied the necessary vehicle. Rather than allowing the Constitution to determine the outcome, the judges selected and extended a line of doctrine until it constitutionalised the outcome they favoured.

Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg's criticism of *Roe* is especially revealing because she supported abortion rights while questioning the constitutional reasoning used to protect them. She argued that the Court had placed the right on unstable foundations and maintained that restrictions on abortion were better understood through the Equal Protection Clause, as restrictions that reinforced women's unequal social and political status [@ginsburg1985; @ginsburg2020]. Her objection was not to the political desirability of abortion rights, but to the constitutional route by which *Roe* had secured them.

Ginsburg did not herself accuse the *Roe* majority of reasoning backwards from a preferred result. Her critique nevertheless strengthens that inference. If even a committed defender of abortion rights found the privacy rationale inadequate and sought a different clause capable of sustaining the same outcome, it becomes harder to maintain that the result followed from any clear constitutional command. The outcome appears stable; the constitutional justification remains interchangeable.

This criticism does not depend on opposition to abortion rights. A person may regard access to abortion as morally important and still deny that judges are entitled to convert that political judgment into constitutional law. The issue is not whether *Roe* reached a desirable policy result. It is whether that result can plausibly be attributed to legal interpretation rather than judicial invention.

If judges may begin with a preferred answer to a divisive social question and then search the Constitution for language broad enough to sustain it, the distinction between adjudication and legislation collapses. The Court is no longer enforcing a decision made by the constitutional text. It is making the decision itself while shielding that decision behind the authority of the Constitution.

That is legislation in substance, and legislation is inherently political. Decisions about whether, when and under what conditions abortion should be lawful require moral judgments, the balancing of competing interests and the drawing of policy lines. Unless the Constitution supplies a sufficiently determinate answer, those choices should be made through the democratic process by representatives who can be held politically accountable.

*Roe* displaced that process by giving one contested political position constitutional status. *Dobbs* exposed how fragile that status had always been: a differently constituted Court, applying the same constitutional text, could withdraw it. The reversal did not show that constitutional meaning had changed. It showed that the earlier rule had depended upon the political judgment of the judges who announced it.

Political scientists have long treated courts as institutions engaged in governance rather than as neutral oracles standing outside politics. Robert Dahl described the Supreme Court as a national policymaking institution, while Martin Shapiro emphasised the multiple political roles performed by courts across different constitutional systems. Critical legal scholars have likewise examined how judicial rhetoric can translate contestable political choices into apparently impersonal legal conclusions [@dahl2001; @shapiro2004; @kennedy1998].

Courts remain indispensable in identifying and enforcing genuine constitutional limits. But they should not manufacture such limits merely because judges consider a particular outcome just. Where the Constitution does not settle the political question, the legislature should not be excluded simply because one side has translated its position into the language of constitutional rights.

## Fatal Bug: Judicial disagreement ignored

Under the existing system, a 9–0 judgment and a 5–4 judgment possess essentially the same formal authority. Both become binding constitutional law.

But the two decisions do not have the same epistemic significance.

When judges with different backgrounds, methodologies and ideological orientations converge upon one conclusion, that agreement is meaningful. A unanimous or nearly unanimous decision gives us some reason to believe that the constitutional question has a relatively determinate legal answer.

A 5–4 or 6–3 judgment tells us something fundamentally different.

It tells us that highly qualified judges, examining the same constitutional text, precedents, historical materials and factual record, have reached competing conclusions. Both sides may produce recognisably legal arguments. Both may appeal to coherent constitutional principles. Neither can plausibly claim that no serious legal dispute exists.

A narrow judicial majority is still a majority. But it is not a legal consensus.

Alexander Bickel famously described judicial review as presenting a “counter-majoritarian difficulty”: unelected judges possess the power to invalidate the decisions of elected institutions. Jeremy Waldron later argued that, under reasonably democratic conditions, persistent disagreement about rights does not disappear merely because it is transferred from legislatures to courts. Judicial voting settles a dispute procedurally, but it does not establish that the majority has discovered the correct answer while the dissenters have failed to understand the law [@bickel1986; @waldron2006].

Here lies an irony at the heart of judicial review. The Court is commonly defended as a remedy for ordinary majoritarian politics: an institution capable of protecting constitutional rights when an electoral majority is unwilling to do so. Yet a 5–4 or 6–3 judgment can reproduce the very majoritarian logic from which the Court is meant to provide refuge. The decisive question becomes not which interpretation is constitutionally correct, but which side has assembled one more vote among nine unelected officials. A bare judicial majority thus replaces a popular majority without resolving the underlying disagreement—or acquiring democratic authority to settle it conclusively.

This problem is especially acute when the judicial majority is narrow. Why should the addition of one judicial vote convert a disputed interpretation into the authoritative meaning of the Constitution for an entire political community?

Why should five unelected officials be entitled to constitutionalise their preferred interpretation, while the disagreement of four equally qualified officials is treated as legally irrelevant?

The conventional answer is that someone must decide. That is correct. But it does not follow that the judiciary must always have the final word.

## Proposal: A Collaborative Model of Constitutional Review

The solution is not to abolish judicial review. Courts remain indispensable to constitutional government. They identify the relevant constitutional provisions, discipline legal argument, interpret precedent, and exclude interpretations that cannot plausibly be reconciled with the constitutional order.

What should be reconsidered is not judicial review, but judicial monopoly over constitutional interpretation.

Constitutional review need not be exercised by the judiciary alone. Where the Constitution admits of a broad legal consensus, courts should have the final word. Where constitutional meaning remains genuinely contested even among judges, however, democratic institutions should participate in resolving that disagreement.

I therefore propose a system of distributed constitutional review.

When the Supreme Court reaches a sufficiently broad consensus—say, at least seven of the nine justices—its interpretation should become the authoritative interpretation of the Constitution. A 9–0, 8–1 or 7–2 judgment would indicate that there is no substantial legal disagreement requiring further democratic resolution.

When the Court divides 6–3 or more narrowly, however, it should no longer impose the preferred interpretation of a narrow judicial majority as the final meaning of the Constitution.

Instead, the justices should prepare two—and only two—constitutional opinions. Before doing so, they should identify and incorporate every proposition on which they agree. **Those shared propositions would become binding constitutional interpretation regardless of what follows**. Only the genuinely disputed questions would be expressed differently in the two opinions.

The two opinions would then be transmitted *verbatim* to the House of Representatives (or to a referendum). The House would not draft its own constitutional interpretation, nor would it be free to invent a third alternative. It would simply choose, by a simple majority, between the two legally defensible interpretations presented by the Court.

The resulting constitutional settlement would derive its authority from two distinct sources.

It would retain *legal legitimacy* of the current model because every available option had been developed and certified by the Supreme Court as constitutionally defensible.

It would also simultaneously possess *democratic legitimacy* because the final choice between those legally defensible alternatives had been made by the chamber that most directly represents the people.

The guiding principle is straightforward:

> Where judges agree, judges should decide. Where judges disagree, democracy should decide between the legally admissible alternatives.

## The Court must first maximise agreement

A constitutional case is not an indivisible object. Judges may agree on most of the relevant legal analysis while disagreeing sharply about only one question.

Suppose that the justices agree on 70 per cent of a case and disagree on the remaining 30 per cent. The propositions on which they agree should appear in both versions of the opinion. Those common propositions would become binding constitutional interpretation regardless of the House’s subsequent vote.

Only the unresolved portion would be submitted for democratic determination.

The Court might agree on the relevant constitutional provision, the historical background, the applicable standard of review and several limits upon governmental authority. It might divide only over whether a particular measure crosses the constitutional boundary.

The House would not be asked to reconsider the agreed legal foundations. Nor would legislators be invited to invent their own constitutional doctrine. They would select only between the two judicially formulated alternatives concerning the unresolved issue.

This feature distinguishes the proposal from simply allowing Congress to overrule judicial decisions.

Congress would not possess a free-standing power to interpret the Constitution however it pleased. The Court would define the range of legally admissible choices. The legislature would exercise political judgment only within that legally certified range.

The requirement that the Court produce no more than two opinions is crucial.

Modern appellate judgments can generate a majority opinion, several concurrences, a principal dissent and multiple partial dissents. The positions may overlap in complex ways, leaving lower courts and public officials uncertain about which propositions command authoritative support.

Under the proposed system, the justices could not externalise every methodological or rhetorical difference in a separate opinion. They would have to negotiate.

They would have to decide which disagreements were fundamental, which were secondary and which could be reconciled through common language. They would be institutionally required to maximise the area of legal agreement before referring the remaining question to democratic judgment.

This would not overburden the House. It would prevent the House from being overburdened.

Legislators would receive two coherent alternatives—not a judicial anthology.

## Incentives for judicial candour

The two-opinion rule would also alter judicial incentives.

A possible objection is that judges might vote strategically in order to trigger or prevent legislative involvement. But the incorporation of judicial consensus into both opinions substantially reduces that incentive.

Because every proposition contained in both opinions would become binding regardless of the House vote, each justice would have an interest in identifying the largest possible area of genuine agreement. Exaggerating disagreement would reduce the body of constitutional doctrine that the Court itself could settle conclusively.

Judges would therefore have reason to distinguish fundamental disagreement from differences of emphasis, preferred methodology or judicial style.

The system would not eliminate strategic behaviour. No institutional design can do that. But it would reward candour more effectively than the present arrangement, under which justices can convert every disagreement into a separate opinion intended to shape future litigation.

Rather than asking nine judges to present themselves as a unified oracle when they are not, the proposal would require them to do something more modest and more useful: identify what they genuinely agree upon and formulate the two strongest legal accounts of what remains disputed.

## Why the House of Representatives?

The final decision should belong to the House of Representatives rather than to Congress as a whole.

The Senate represents states as states. Its allocation of two senators to every state serves a federal purpose, but it does not embody political equality among citizens. A Senate majority can represent substantially fewer voters than the opposing minority.

That may be defensible in areas where the federal balance itself is constitutionally relevant. It is a less persuasive arrangement for resolving a question referred to democratic judgment precisely because legal institutions could not settle it.

The House is not perfectly representative. District boundaries, electoral rules and geographic concentrations of voters all affect its composition. Nevertheless, it is the federal chamber whose organising principle is representation by population. It is also more electorally responsive because all its members face election every two years.

If constitutional ambiguity is to be resolved through popular sovereignty, the House is therefore the more appropriate institution.

The choice of the House also places this proposal within—but does not simply reproduce—the tradition of popular constitutionalism. Larry Kramer has argued that constitutional meaning should not be treated as the exclusive possession of courts and that the Constitution ultimately belongs to “the people themselves”. Richard Bellamy’s political constitutionalism similarly defends electoral competition, political equality and majority decision-making as constitutional mechanisms rather than as threats from which constitutionalism must be protected (Kramer 2004; Bellamy 2007).

My proposal gives these democratic principles a more structured institutional form. The House would not replace legal judgment; it would complete a process that legal judgment alone had been unable to conclude.

## Why a simple majority?

It may seem natural to require a supermajority in the House. Constitutional questions are important, the argument goes, and should not be resolved by a narrow or temporary political coalition.

But a supermajority requirement would undermine the central purpose of the proposal.

If the House selects one interpretation by 218 votes to 217, that is not merely an unfortunate procedural outcome. It is substantive information. It tells us that democratic support for the prevailing interpretation is weak, just as the divided Court told us that its legal support was weak.

The narrowness of the result should remain visible.

The current system performs a strange constitutional alchemy. A question may divide judges, citizens and elected officials. Yet once five justices form a majority, the contested position is presented as settled constitutional law. The weakness of its support disappears behind the authority of judicial office.

My proposal refuses that transformation.

If an interpretation possesses narrow legal support and narrow democratic support, the constitutional settlement should be recognised as narrow. It must govern for the time being because a decision has to be made, but it should remain open to reconsideration after subsequent elections.

A simple majority prevents deadlock. It also allows constitutional interpretation to respond when democratic opinion changes.

This does not mean that every constitutional rule should fluctuate with each electoral cycle. Rules supported by substantial judicial consensus would remain insulated from ordinary legislative revision. Democratic revisability would apply only to questions on which the Supreme Court itself had certified the existence of serious legal disagreement.

Where the law is relatively determinate, stability is justified. Where the law remains deeply contested, judicial entrenchment may merely protect the preferences of the judges who happened to form a majority at a particular moment.

Instability among voters is routinely treated as a constitutional danger. Instability among judges is concealed by giving the winning judicial coalition the final word.

There is no obvious reason to privilege the latter.

## What about minority rights?

The strongest objection is that democratic majorities cannot always be trusted to protect minorities. Judicial review is frequently defended on the ground that courts can protect rights that unpopular groups cannot defend through ordinary politics.

This objection must be taken seriously. Democracy is not reducible to majority rule, and constitutional rights should constrain the mistreatment or exclusion of minorities.

But it does not follow that a narrow majority of judges is systematically more reliable than a democratic legislature.

Courts have sometimes protected vulnerable groups. They have also upheld slavery, racial segregation, forced sterilisation, restrictions on labour organisation and other forms of domination. Judicial independence can insulate principled decisions from public pressure, but it can equally insulate judicial error from democratic correction.

Waldron’s critique of judicial review does not deny the importance of rights; it questions whether disagreement about rights is necessarily resolved more legitimately by majority voting among judges than by majority voting among elected representatives. John Hart Ely’s influential defence of judicial review, meanwhile, focused not upon judges selecting society’s substantive values but upon correcting failures in democratic representation and protecting political participation (Waldron 2006; Ely 1980).

Conditional judicial supremacy preserves a substantial judicial role.

The Court would exclude interpretations falling outside the range of legally defensible constitutional argument. Its areas of broad consensus would remain binding. Only where at least a substantial minority of justices endorsed an alternative would the House become involved.

The democratic branch would therefore not be choosing between constitutionalism and lawlessness. It would be choosing between two positions that the Court itself had certified as serious constitutional interpretations.

The proposal cannot guarantee substantively just outcomes. Neither can the present system.

Its more defensible promise is procedural: where neither legal expertise nor judicial deliberation produces a clear answer, the final choice should be made by an institution that citizens can replace.

## Beyond strong-form judicial review

This proposal belongs to a wider body of scholarship challenging the assumption that courts must always possess the final word on constitutional meaning.

Mark Tushnet distinguishes American-style “strong-form” judicial review from weaker arrangements in which legislatures retain greater capacity to respond to judicial decisions. Peter Hogg and Allison Bushell describe the Canadian system as a “dialogue” between courts and legislatures, while Rosalind Dixon’s account of responsive judicial review asks courts to recognise the limits of their own institutional capacity and legitimacy. Ran Hirschl, from a different perspective, has examined the global transfer of political power towards courts as a movement towards “juristocracy” (Hogg and Bushell 1997; Hirschl 2004; Tushnet 2008; Dixon 2023).

My proposal shares with these approaches a rejection of absolute judicial supremacy, but differs in three respects.

First, legislative involvement would be triggered by the **degree of judicial disagreement**. The vote split would be treated as information about the determinacy of constitutional law.

Second, the Court would have a formal duty to separate consensus from disagreement and to incorporate the consensus into both opinions.

Third, the House could choose only between two alternatives formulated by the justices. Democratic judgment would be neither unlimited nor merely consultative.

The proposal thus combines legal and political constitutionalism rather than selecting one against the other.

Judicial institutions would define the boundaries of constitutional legality. Democratic institutions would choose within those boundaries when legal reasoning produced more than one defensible answer.

## The politics hidden inside expertise

The mystification of constitutional judgment resembles a wider tendency in modern governance: political choices are often placed in the hands of experts and then redescribed as technical necessities.

Central bankers, for example, possess specialised knowledge of monetary operations, financial systems and inflation dynamics. But expertise alone cannot determine how much unemployment society should tolerate, whose income should bear the cost of disinflation, or whether price stability should take priority over other social objectives.

Those are distributive and political choices, even when they are expressed through technical models.

Constitutional adjudication presents an analogous problem.

Judges possess specialised legal knowledge. They can identify precedent, reconstruct doctrine, interpret texts and articulate principles more systematically than most legislators. But expertise cannot always determine which conception of liberty, equality, citizenship or executive authority should prevail when several interpretations remain legally plausible.

Political judgment does not cease to be political because it appears in a judicial opinion.

Footnotes are not holy water.

Expertise should structure public choice. It should not be used to conceal public choice.

## Unpacking the veil

The public feuding described by the *Financial Times* is likely to be interpreted as a threat to the Supreme Court’s legitimacy. A court whose members openly exchange ideological and personal accusations may struggle to preserve an appearance of detached authority.

But perhaps that appearance itself deserves scrutiny.

The problem is not simply that judges have recently become too political. It is that constitutional institutions have long relied upon denying how much political judgment difficult constitutional cases require.

The justices disagree about citizenship, equality, liberty, executive authority and democracy. These disagreements are not necessarily accidental intrusions into an otherwise mechanical legal process. They often reflect conflicts embedded within the constitutional order itself.

The answer cannot simply be to restore the holy atmosphere by encouraging the justices to become more polite.

Civility is desirable, but politeness does not turn disagreement into legal certainty. A restrained 5–4 judgment remains a 5–4 judgment. Robes, marble columns and ceremonial language do not change its epistemic status.

Rather than repairing the veil, we should unpack it.

We should identify which parts of constitutional adjudication rest upon broad legal agreement and which depend upon contested political judgment. Judicial authority should be strongest in the former category. Democratic authority should become decisive in the latter.

## A more honest constitutionalism

Judges are not holy. Constitutions do not speak independently of the human beings who interpret them. Law can constrain political choice, but it cannot always eliminate it.

A constitutional system should acknowledge this rather than disguising judicial disagreement as constitutional certainty.

Where the Supreme Court reaches broad agreement, its interpretation should be final.

Where the Court remains substantially divided, the justices should identify their common ground, formulate two coherent alternatives and send those alternatives to the House of Representatives.

The House should decide between them by a simple majority.

If the vote is narrow, the public should see that it is narrow. If democratic opinion changes after a subsequent election, the interpretation should remain open to revision. A constitutional question that lacks both legal consensus and durable political support should not be permanently removed from democratic life by a professional legal elite.

This is not an argument against legal expertise. It is an argument about its proper limits.

Judges should determine which interpretations are constitutionally defensible.

They should not always determine which defensible interpretation must govern everyone else.

Once the constitutional veil has been unpacked, the division of labour becomes clearer:

> Law should define the alternatives.
> Where law cannot choose between them, democracy should decide.

---

## References

Bellamy, Richard. 2007. *Political Constitutionalism: A Republican Defence of the Constitutionality of Democracy*. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

Dixon, Rosalind. 2023. *Responsive Judicial Review: Democracy and Dysfunction in the Modern Age*. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

Ely, John Hart. 1980. *Democracy and Distrust: A Theory of Judicial Review*. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press.

Hirschl, Ran. 2004. *Towards Juristocracy: The Origins and Consequences of the New Constitutionalism*. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press.

Hogg, Peter W., and Allison A. Bushell. 1997. “The Charter Dialogue between Courts and Legislatures.” *Osgoode Hall Law Journal* 35(1): 75–124.

Kramer, Larry D. 2004. *The People Themselves: Popular Constitutionalism and Judicial Review*. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

Tushnet, Mark. 2008. *Weak Courts, Strong Rights: Judicial Review and Social Welfare Rights in Comparative Constitutional Law*. Princeton: Princeton University Press.

## References
